category.name: Failure Modes
category.summary: Named software-engineering failure patterns with warning signs, escalation paths, recovery moves, and AI-era distortions.

entries.title: The Friendly Rewrite
entries.summary: A rewrite framed as cleanup becomes a long-running replacement with no stable landing zone.
entries.whatItIs: A full or near-full rewrite justified by pain in the current system, but without a disciplined migration strategy.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before the rewrite becomes the default answer
looksHealthyBecause: the new system uses current frameworks and cleaner patterns
architecture diagrams look tidier than the old code
leadership hears the words modernization, scalability, and debt reduction
morale is visibly higher on the rewrite side of the team

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Migration debt is about unfinished prior moves. The friendly rewrite is a new move whose endpoint was never defined.
Refactoring preserves behavior and scope. The friendly rewrite expands scope while the old system stays live.
Abstraction addiction adds layers inside one system. The friendly rewrite builds a parallel system.

entries.starts: The current system is painful. Everyone agrees. A clean slate feels rational, overdue, even virtuous.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: The existing mess is real, and a rewrite promises clarity, speed, and fewer compromises.
entries.escalates: Parallel systems appear. Scope expands. The new system is always almost ready while the old one remains mission-critical.
entries.ends: The team maintains two worlds, trust declines, and leadership eventually asks why nothing meaningful shipped.

early: We should rebuild this properly.
The old system is too messy to change safely.
No one can describe what will be migrated first.
mid: Two systems run in parallel for months.
Net-new scope gets added before parity is reached.
Delivery dates keep moving but optimism stays strangely intact.
late: The old system still receives urgent fixes.
Nobody can define done in plain language.
The team has lost confidence in both systems.

whyItHappens: Pain in the current system is real
Migration strategy is weak or absent
Teams underestimate hidden behavior in existing systems
Ambition grows faster than discipline
Leadership likes the symbolism of a fresh start
immediateActions: Freeze net-new scope until migration strategy is explicit
Define smallest valuable slice to replace first
Write down exit criteria for each migrated area
structuralFixes: Use strangler migration patterns
Make cutover milestones concrete and testable
Track parity, usage, and retirement explicitly
whatNotToDo: Do not let the rewrite absorb unrelated improvements
Do not treat morale as evidence of viability
entries.firstMove: name the rewrite as a rewrite in writing, and stop framing it as cleanup.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept that some of the new system will be thrown away, or accept that the old system will outlive the migration by years.
entries.recoveryTrap: launching a second rewrite to fix the first rewrite.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help inventory legacy behavior, map dependencies, generate migration checklists, and summarize hidden code paths.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can accelerate the wrong rewrite by generating scaffolding and broad rewrites faster than the team can validate architecture and migration risk.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated replacement code looks reviewed because it compiles and passes shallow tests, creating the illusion that behavior has been validated when only surface shape has.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Do not confuse generated replacement code with validated replacement behavior.
entries.commonQuote: We'll rewrite it properly this time.
entries.counterMove: Replace one slice, prove one cutover, retire one dependency.
entries.falsePositive: Some rewrites are justified; the failure mode is not rewriting, but rewriting without disciplined displacement.

entries.title: The Hero Trap
entries.summary: One person becomes the informal system of record for critical knowledge, decisions, and rescue work.
entries.whatItIs: A team relies on one trusted person to keep delivery, systems, or decisions moving.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when dependence is admired but before it becomes normalized
looksHealthyBecause: one person consistently delivers under pressure
the team publicly thanks them for saving launches and incidents
leadership cites them as a model of ownership
velocity numbers look fine while they are on call

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Expertise is a property of the person. The hero trap is a property of the team: the work only moves when that person is present.
Ownership drift is diffuse and unclear. The hero trap is concentrated and over-clear - everything routes to one name.
A quiet-quitter team disengages across the board. A hero-trap team is highly engaged with one person carrying most of the load.

entries.starts: Someone capable, generous, and fast becomes the default path for hard work.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: The team gets quick answers, fast fixes, and a comforting sense that someone truly understands the system.
entries.escalates: Escalations route through one person. Others stop learning certain areas. Documentation falls behind reality.
entries.ends: Burnout, bottlenecks, fragile delivery, and organizational panic when the hero is absent.

early: Ask Alex, they know.
The same person joins every critical call.
Some system areas have one real maintainer.
mid: Work queues form behind one person.
People avoid risky changes without their sign-off.
Key knowledge exists mostly in chats and memory.
late: Vacations become operational risk.
Progress stalls when one person is overloaded.
The team has strong output but weak resilience.

whyItHappens: Capability and trust concentrate naturally
Organizations reward rescue more than resilience
Knowledge sharing is treated as optional overhead
Managers tolerate or celebrate single-threaded expertise
immediateActions: Map critical areas with single-person dependency
Pair on risky domains immediately
Redirect urgent requests away from the same person by design
structuralFixes: Rotate ownership
Publish service ownership and backup ownership
Require decision summaries and runbooks for critical areas
whatNotToDo: Do not shame the hero for organizational dependency
Do not call it solved because some docs were written once
entries.firstMove: route the next critical incident deliberately away from the hero, with an explicit second owner named in advance.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept slower response times and more visible near-misses in the short term so that other engineers build real context.
entries.recoveryTrap: writing a large documentation push and declaring the knowledge transferred.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help externalize tacit knowledge into diagrams, docs, FAQs, runbooks, and onboarding material.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can deepen the trap if one senior person becomes the only person trusted to validate generated code or AI-produced recommendations.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated docs and summaries based on the hero's past work create the illusion that their knowledge has been captured, when what was captured is shape, not judgment.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated explanations based on stale or partial context can create the illusion that knowledge has been transferred when it has not.
entries.commonQuote: It's faster if Sam just does it.
entries.counterMove: Turn rescue knowledge into team knowledge while delivery still works.
entries.falsePositive: Experts are not the problem. Unshared criticality is.

entries.title: Abstraction Addiction
entries.summary: The system grows more layers, indirection, and generic structure than current reality actually demands.
entries.whatItIs: A codebase accumulates abstractions to solve anticipated future problems rather than repeated present ones.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when the abstraction is proposed, not after it spreads
looksHealthyBecause: the team cites SOLID, DDD, hexagonal, or similar vocabularies fluently
architecture diagrams look cleaner than the actual code experience
pull requests come with thoughtful rationale about future flexibility
senior engineers endorse the direction as forward-looking

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Platform-before-product builds infrastructure ahead of demand. Abstraction addiction adds structure inside a product that already has users.
Future-proofing responds to a concrete, likely next change. Abstraction addiction responds to generalized anxiety about unknown future changes.
A friendly rewrite replaces the system. Abstraction addiction keeps the system but multiplies its internal layers.

entries.starts: A team wants flexibility, consistency, and elegance.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Generalization sounds cheaper than revisiting design later, and abstraction often signals sophistication.
entries.escalates: Interfaces multiply, wrappers appear, patterns spread, and the code becomes harder to reason about than the original problem.
entries.ends: Simple changes take too long, onboarding slows, and engineers stop trusting the architecture.

early: We might need this pattern later.
A simple feature requires several layers from day one.
Names become increasingly generic.
mid: Simple changes touch too many files.
The abstraction has more exceptions than the concrete implementation it replaced.
Developers ask why the code is so indirect.
late: Nobody can explain which layer owns the real behavior.
Refactors are feared because the architecture feels ceremonial.
Reuse is promised more often than observed.

whyItHappens: Teams optimize for elegance over clarity
Architecture is used to manage uncertainty symbolically
Past pain makes teams overcompensate
Patterns spread socially faster than evidence accumulates
immediateActions: Identify layers that do not carry distinct business or operational value
Trace one simple feature end to end and count unnecessary hops
Demand concrete use cases for proposed abstractions
structuralFixes: Prefer duplication over premature indirection
Require repeated evidence before generalizing
Run architecture reviews focused on clarity, not cleverness
whatNotToDo: Do not declare war on abstraction itself
Do not replace one grand framework with another grand framework
entries.firstMove: pick one simple feature, trace it end to end, and count every layer that does not change outcomes if removed.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept local duplication and a less elegant codebase so that the cost of change drops where it matters.
entries.recoveryTrap: building a new grand abstraction to unify the existing abstractions.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help identify duplication patterns worth abstracting and map dead or redundant layers for simplification.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can mass-produce interfaces, helper layers, patterns, and generic architecture that look polished but add no real leverage.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated layers look intentional because they follow named patterns and pass lint, creating the illusion of considered design where no real design decision was made.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated elegance is still cost. If no one can explain why a layer exists, it is already too expensive.
entries.commonQuote: We'll need this flexibility later.
entries.counterMove: Make the simple path simple again.
entries.falsePositive: Some abstractions are essential. The failure mode begins when abstraction outruns evidence.

entries.title: Ticket Theater
entries.summary: Work tracking becomes performance for stakeholders instead of coordination for delivery.
entries.whatItIs: Boards, statuses, and progress rituals become more important than real flow, learning, or shipping.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when process starts producing confidence without improving outcomes
looksHealthyBecause: boards are tidy, dashboards are current, and statuses move every day
leadership can get a clean weekly update with one click
the team speaks in consistent process vocabulary
external reviewers praise the visibility

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Delivery discipline uses the board to coordinate real work. Ticket theater uses the board to make the work look coordinated.
Metric myopia optimizes the wrong numbers. Ticket theater optimizes the wrong artifacts - statuses, titles, and visible motion.
Discovery theater performs learning before build. Ticket theater performs progress during build.

entries.starts: A team wants visibility and control.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Tickets, statuses, and dashboards create an immediate sense of order.
entries.escalates: The system of reporting becomes heavier, and teams learn which motions look like progress.
entries.ends: Leadership sees movement, teams feel burden, and delivery quietly deteriorates.

early: Status hygiene gets more attention than blocker removal.
Tickets are split for optics rather than flow.
People speak in board language more than delivery language.
mid: A lot changes state, but little reaches users.
The team spends significant time curating work representation.
Metrics improve while real predictability does not.
late: Teams optimize for appearances under review.
Stakeholders are surprised despite heavy reporting.
Nobody trusts the board, but everyone feeds it.

whyItHappens: Visibility is substituted for control
Low trust drives reporting inflation
Proxy metrics become performance targets
Leaders want certainty from inherently uncertain work
immediateActions: Remove statuses that do not change decisions
Review actual cycle time and blocked time
Ask which meetings and fields nobody would miss
structuralFixes: Measure outcomes and flow, not just artifact movement
Shorten feedback loops to real usage or acceptance
Use the board to coordinate, not to perform
whatNotToDo: Do not abolish process without replacing it with better operating habits
Do not add another dashboard to fix dashboard distortion
entries.firstMove: pick one weekly report or status ritual and remove it for two weeks; see what decisions actually change.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept that leadership will see less apparent movement in exchange for more honest signal on what is stuck.
entries.recoveryTrap: replacing the old board with a new tool or framework and calling the theater fixed.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can summarize real blockers, cluster delivery risks, and reduce administrative overhead when used against noise rather than for more noise.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate persuasive updates, summaries, and ticket churn at scale, making synthetic clarity feel like real control.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated status summaries read as crisp and confident, creating the illusion that the team has a handle on the work when all that improved was the writing.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: If AI makes reporting cheaper, organizations may consume more reporting instead of demanding better signal.
entries.commonQuote: Can we just make sure the board reflects reality?
entries.counterMove: Use reality to simplify the board, not the board to redefine reality.
entries.falsePositive: Well-run ticket systems are useful. The failure mode begins when representation outranks delivery.

entries.title: The Invisible Deadline
entries.summary: A date exists socially or politically, but not explicitly enough for the team to manage the trade-offs honestly.
entries.whatItIs: A commitment is understood by powerful people, but scope, quality, and dependencies were never aligned around it explicitly.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when urgency is felt but still vaguely framed
looksHealthyBecause: there is energy and urgency in the team
stakeholders talk about the goal with confidence
roadmaps and leadership slides mention a target window
nobody is openly panicking yet

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: A hard deadline is explicit, owned, and tied to traceable trade-offs. An invisible deadline is implied, unowned, and never forced anyone to choose.
Scope-negotiation theater performs trade-off conversations that change nothing. The invisible deadline is the absence of those conversations entirely.
Stakeholder capture means one voice dominates priorities. The invisible deadline means timing was absorbed without any explicit priority conversation at all.

entries.starts: A launch window, stakeholder expectation, or executive assumption emerges without precise negotiation.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: No one wants to be the person who slows momentum by forcing hard trade-off conversations.
entries.escalates: The team senses urgency but lacks clear constraints. Local optimism fills the gap.
entries.ends: The deadline becomes suddenly visible only when panic arrives.

early: We should aim for around then.
People talk about timing without scope conversations.
Urgency is implied, not owned.
mid: Roadmaps sound precise but delivery plans stay vague.
Trade-offs are made informally and unevenly.
People are surprised by the seriousness of the date.
late: Escalation tone changes abruptly.
Quality is cut in a rush.
Teams discover they have been committed without knowing it.

whyItHappens: Conflict avoidance
Leadership signaling without operational translation
Wishful alignment
Unclear decision rights around commitments
immediateActions: Name the date explicitly
Tie it to scope, quality, and dependency assumptions
Ask what will be cut, deferred, or protected
structuralFixes: Use explicit commitment rituals
Record decision owners and trade-offs
Review deadline assumptions early and often
whatNotToDo: Do not respond with heroic optimism
Do not pretend ambiguity is flexibility
entries.firstMove: write the date on paper with the decision-maker's name next to it, and send it back up the chain for confirmation or correction.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept the discomfort of a hard conversation now, or accept a surprise escalation later when the date becomes real.
entries.recoveryTrap: taking the implied date at face value and asking the team to absorb scope quietly to hit it.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can detect vague commitments across notes, plans, and status updates and surface mismatched assumptions.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make progress reports sound calm and coherent, delaying the moment when underlying ambiguity is confronted.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-polished updates read as measured and coherent, creating the illusion that the plan is well-understood when nothing about the actual commitment has been sharpened.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A polished summary is not a clarified commitment.
entries.commonQuote: We're not committing yet, but it would be good to have it by Q3.
entries.counterMove: Say the date out loud, then make the cost visible.
entries.falsePositive: Not every target date is unhealthy. The issue is hidden commitment, not commitment itself.

entries.title: Stakeholder Capture
entries.summary: A team's direction gets distorted by one loud stakeholder's agenda at the expense of broader product coherence.
entries.whatItIs: A single stakeholder's preferences or urgency persistently reshapes roadmap, scope, or architecture beyond their legitimate influence.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when priorities shift rapidly in response to one voice rather than user or business evidence
looksHealthyBecause: leadership endorses the team as responsive and aligned
requests flow cleanly from leadership into the backlog
status updates emphasize shipped executive priorities
the loudest stakeholder calls the team high-performing

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Consensus trap stalls decisions waiting for broad agreement. Stakeholder capture moves quickly - it just moves wherever one voice points.
Scope-negotiation theater performs trade-off conversations without real cuts. Stakeholder capture skips the trade-off entirely.
Healthy input informs direction. Capture displaces it.

entries.starts: A powerful stakeholder has a strong opinion and the team accommodates it.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Responsiveness to leadership feels like good organizational citizenship.
entries.escalates: The team learns that one voice moves priorities faster than any process. Others exploit the same channel.
entries.ends: Roadmaps become incoherent, teams are demoralized by churn, and actual users are underserved.

early: Roadmaps swing fast after one meeting.
Priorities keep reinterpreting without documented rationale.
One person's requests bypass normal intake.
mid: The team cannot explain current priorities in terms of user or business need.
Previous commitments are quietly dropped.
Team energy drops around planning cycles.
late: Churn is constant and expected.
The team stops believing roadmaps are real.
Key people start leaving.

whyItHappens: Power imbalance
Unclear decision rights
Fear-driven prioritization
Absence of a product strategy that can absorb pressure
immediateActions: Document the request and its impact on existing commitments
Make the displaced work visible before accepting new work
Escalate trade-offs explicitly rather than absorbing them silently
structuralFixes: Define decision rights and prioritization criteria
Record trade-offs with named owners
Use a product strategy document as a stable reference point
whatNotToDo: Do not absorb new scope without naming what it displaces
Do not rely on the stakeholder to self-limit
entries.firstMove: write the request down alongside everything it displaces on the current plan, and send that paper back to the requester before executing.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept the political cost of visibly pushing back on one stakeholder, or accept the coherence cost of a product shaped by whoever was loudest last.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding more stakeholders to the intake process, which makes capture negotiable but does not end it.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can summarize trade-offs and show impact of changes more objectively, providing a neutral view of what changes across scenarios.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can produce persuasive but shallow artifacts that reinforce the loudest voice by generating fast, polished justifications for whatever direction was most recently requested.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-produced justifications for the latest requested direction arrive polished and fast, creating the illusion that the pivot is well-reasoned when reasoning is exactly what was skipped.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Speed of justification is not quality of reasoning.
entries.commonQuote: Leadership really wants this done first.
entries.counterMove: Show what gets displaced before agreeing to what gets added.
entries.falsePositive: Not all executive input is capture. The failure mode is persistent distortion, not occasional override.

entries.title: The Dependency Fog
entries.summary: Critical upstream and downstream dependencies exist, but are discovered too late and managed too weakly.
entries.whatItIs: A team believes it understands the path to delivery, but hidden dependencies keep changing that path after work has started.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before sequencing work based on assumptions
looksHealthyBecause: each team can describe its own work crisply
dependencies are acknowledged informally by everyone involved
planning meetings produce confident delivery dates
nobody has flagged a blocker yet

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That failure mode is about the shape of specific contracts drifting. Dependency fog is about the set of dependencies being unknown at all.
Ownership drift is unclear who owns a system. Dependency fog is unclear who agreed to deliver what to whom.
Real coordination has named commitments. Fog has shared assumptions.

entries.starts: The plan looks achievable from the perspective of each individual team.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Dependencies are often socially known but not operationally managed.
entries.escalates: Integration assumptions fail, handoffs slip, and blockers appear serially instead of visibly.
entries.ends: Schedules slide, blame circulates, and teams discover they never had a shared delivery map.

early: We'll need something from that team later.
Dependencies live mostly in conversations.
Integration points are assumed rather than tested.
mid: Blockers keep appearing after planning.
Teams report progress locally but not systemically.
Release plans depend on unowned external promises.
late: Everyone says they are waiting on everyone else.
Cross-team integration becomes a fire drill.
Dates move because unknowns arrive as surprises.

whyItHappens: Local planning dominates system planning
Ownership boundaries are unclear
Interfaces are socially assumed, not technically or operationally validated
Cross-team coordination is underinvested
immediateActions: Map dependencies explicitly
Name owners, dates, and integration proof points
Surface assumptions in writing
structuralFixes: Track critical path dependencies separately
Add early integration milestones
Use program-level visibility only where it changes action
whatNotToDo: Do not treat every dependency as equal
Do not wait for full certainty before testing interfaces
entries.firstMove: list every external input your next milestone depends on - with an owner, a date, and a written confirmation from that owner.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept slower commitments now in exchange for commitments that actually hold, or keep the fog and keep missing dates.
entries.recoveryTrap: building a dependency-tracking tool before anyone has started owning dependencies by name.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can extract dependency graphs from tickets, docs, code, and meeting notes and make critical paths more visible.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can make local work appear faster and cleaner while hiding the fact that system-wide dependency uncertainty remains unresolved.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated dependency maps look authoritative because the diagrams are clean, creating the illusion that dependencies are managed when they have only been inventoried.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Dependency maps are only useful if they are acted on, not just generated.
entries.commonQuote: We should be unblocked once the other team lands their part.
entries.counterMove: Turn vague dependencies into owned, testable commitments.
entries.falsePositive: Complex work really does have dependencies. The failure mode is unmanaged dependency, not dependency itself.

entries.title: Local Optimization
entries.summary: One team improves its own metric while creating or worsening bottlenecks in the wider system.
entries.whatItIs: A team's performance on its own metrics improves, but the changes harm or ignore system-wide flow and outcomes.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when team metrics are celebrated without checking downstream impact
looksHealthyBecause: each team's own metrics move in the right direction
team leads can point to clear improvements under their control
reviews celebrate per-team efficiency gains
retrospectives focus on what each team shipped

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Metric myopia is measuring the wrong thing. Local optimization is optimizing a local thing well while the system degrades.
Feature factory ships without learning. Local optimization ships efficiently while the end-to-end outcome gets worse.
Autonomy lets teams own their work. Local optimization lets teams own their metric while disowning the handoff.

entries.starts: A team is given a metric and improves it.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Teams are meant to own their numbers and improve them.
entries.escalates: Downstream teams absorb the cost invisibly. The handoff gets worse. Queue lengths grow elsewhere.
entries.ends: The system slows while each team reports improvement.

early: Team celebrates throughput while downstream pain rises.
Metrics are team-level, not end-to-end.
Handoffs are not tracked.
mid: Queue length or wait time grows in adjacent teams.
Downstream teams describe getting too much, too fast, or in the wrong shape.
System-level lead time is not improving despite local gains.
late: Incidents arise at team boundaries.
No one owns the full flow.
Each team defends its numbers while the product suffers.

whyItHappens: Silo incentives
Poor system-level metrics
Local autonomy without coordination
Goodhart's Law applied at team level
immediateActions: Map end-to-end flow from request to user value
Identify where work queues between teams
Add a cross-team outcome metric alongside local metrics
structuralFixes: Align incentives to end-to-end outcomes
Make handoff quality a first-class metric
Review system flow in addition to team velocity
whatNotToDo: Do not remove local metrics entirely
Do not assume good local numbers mean a healthy system
entries.firstMove: trace one real user-value flow end to end, and measure total time through every team boundary - not each team's internal cycle time.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept that some team metrics will stop improving (or visibly regress) so the system can improve.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding a cross-team metric on top of the existing team metrics, which creates reporting work without changing incentives.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can reveal system-wide flow bottlenecks if connected to data across team boundaries.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can hyper-optimize local workflows and make teams look even more efficient in isolation, deepening fragmentation.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI makes each team's local dashboards more compelling than ever, deepening the illusion that local excellence is system excellence.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Optimizing parts independently does not optimize the whole.
entries.commonQuote: Our team's velocity is up 20% this quarter.
entries.whoSaysThis: engineering manager in a planning review
entries.counterMove: Measure the handoff, not just the output.
entries.falsePositive: Strong team metrics are not the problem. Metrics that ignore system impact are.

entries.title: The Consensus Trap
entries.summary: Decision-making slows or stalls as the team seeks broad agreement that never fully arrives.
entries.whatItIs: Important decisions circulate through too many people with no clear owner, producing delay or uncommitted half-decisions.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when a decision has been discussed more than twice without a named owner
looksHealthyBecause: everyone had a chance to speak
the team culture reads as inclusive and thoughtful
decisions are not being rushed
no one is visibly unhappy with the outcome

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Quiet-quitter harmony is disengagement. Consensus trap is over-engagement without closure.
Scope theater performs cuts that aren't made. Consensus trap performs agreement that isn't reached.
Deliberation converges. A trap keeps circling.

entries.starts: A team wants buy-in and avoids making decisions that others might contest.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Involving people feels respectful and reduces the risk of pushback later.
entries.escalates: Every decision needs more input. Proposals get softened. Ownership stays fuzzy.
entries.ends: Stale proposals, missed windows, and quiet disengagement from the people who most wanted to move.

early: Endless reviews with no clear decider.
Proposals get progressively softer.
People say we need to align more before proceeding.
mid: Decisions made in one meeting get reopened in the next.
No one can name who owns a key choice.
Work waits on clarity that never comes.
late: Decisions are made by attrition or accident.
The team executes on the least-contested option, not the best one.
Strong contributors disengage from the decision process.

whyItHappens: Unclear decision rights
Conflict avoidance
Over-collaboration norms
Fear of being wrong publicly
immediateActions: Name a decision owner before the next discussion
Time-box input: a fixed period for consultation, then a decision
Separate consultation from approval explicitly
structuralFixes: Use a DRI (directly responsible individual) model
Make decision records part of normal delivery hygiene
Distinguish decisions that need consensus from decisions that need awareness
whatNotToDo: Do not confuse consultation with consent
Do not make every decision a group decision
entries.firstMove: name a decision owner with a deadline before the next meeting, and state clearly that after the deadline the owner decides alone.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept someone being unhappy with the outcome, or accept that the decision will not happen.
entries.recoveryTrap: introducing a formal decision framework without naming a decision owner under it.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can summarize positions and surface genuine disagreement crisply, reducing the surface area of the decision.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can multiply plausible arguments for every position, making consensus feel even further away.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated meeting notes and decision logs make the absence of closure look procedurally sound - the artifact trail implies deliberation happened even when no one actually chose.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: More arguments do not mean better decisions.
entries.commonQuote: We just need a bit more alignment before we move forward.
entries.counterMove: Assign an owner before scheduling another discussion.
entries.falsePositive: Some decisions genuinely require consensus. The failure mode is treating all decisions this way.

entries.title: Metric Myopia
entries.summary: Teams overvalue what is measurable and systematically underweight what actually matters.
entries.whatItIs: Proxy metrics displace real outcomes as the primary signal for team and organizational health.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when improving a metric stops correlating with improving the actual outcome
looksHealthyBecause: the team looks quantitative and disciplined
dashboards and reviews feature prominently
leadership sees concrete movement week over week
external reviewers praise the data culture

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Test theater is metric myopia about correctness. Metric myopia is the broader pattern across any domain.
Ticket theater optimizes for board-state. Metric myopia optimizes for the wrong signal.
Data-driven management uses numbers alongside outcomes. Myopia uses numbers instead of outcomes.

entries.starts: The organization wants measurable accountability and installs dashboards.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Quantified goals feel objective and fair.
entries.escalates: Teams learn that the metric moves independently of the outcome. Gaming begins, usually unconsciously at first.
entries.ends: Metrics improve, real performance does not, and management is the last to see it.

early: Dashboards dominate planning conversations.
Nuanced concerns get dismissed if they are not in the numbers.
Teams talk about the metric more than the user.
mid: Metric is improving but incidents, complaints, or churn are not falling.
People work around measurement rather than around delivery.
Counter-metrics go unmeasured.
late: The metric has been gamed sufficiently that it no longer signals anything.
Real problems are visible to frontline teams but invisible to leadership.
Morale drops even as numbers look good.

whyItHappens: Proxy metrics become targets per Goodhart's Law
Measurement is detached from reality
Leadership prefers legible data to ambiguous truth
Counter-metrics are not tracked alongside primary metrics
immediateActions: Pair each primary metric with a counter-metric
Add qualitative signal alongside quantitative dashboards
Ask what the metric would miss if it were being gamed
structuralFixes: Tie metrics to user or business outcomes, not internal activity
Review metrics against real incidents and outcomes quarterly
Rotate which metrics receive executive attention
whatNotToDo: Do not abolish measurement
Do not add more metrics to fix metric distortion
entries.firstMove: pair the primary metric with a counter-metric that would regress if the primary were being gamed, and publish both side by side.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept lower metric performance temporarily while the team recalibrates what the number is supposed to track.
entries.recoveryTrap: replacing the bad metric with a composite score that smooths the distortion without removing it.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help correlate metrics with real incidents and outcomes, surfacing disconnects between measures and reality.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can turbocharge metric performance with endless analytics and polished dashboards that make proxy measures look authoritative.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated narratives explaining metric movement make proxy-wins sound insightful, lending the dashboard story a confidence the underlying measurement does not deserve.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A confident-looking dashboard is not an honest one.
entries.commonQuote: The numbers look great this quarter.
entries.whoSaysThis: senior leader in a business review
entries.counterMove: Add a counter-metric before celebrating the primary one.
entries.falsePositive: Some metrics are genuinely useful. Myopia begins when the metric displaces the outcome it was meant to represent.

entries.title: Platform Before Product
entries.summary: Internal platform investment grows significantly beyond proven user or product need.
entries.whatItIs: A team or organization builds a platform, framework, or internal tool before demonstrating repeatable demand from multiple real users.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before platform work becomes a multi-team or multi-quarter investment
looksHealthyBecause: the platform has sophisticated design docs and a roadmap
engineers talk fluently about platform abstractions
leadership hears "leverage for future teams"
early adopters inside the platform team are enthusiastic

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Premature scaling builds for load that hasn't arrived. This is platform built for users that haven't arrived.
Abstraction addiction adds layers inside one product. This builds a whole product-shaped thing ahead of a product.
Legitimate platforms serve real, named internal demand. This one serves anticipated demand.

entries.starts: A team spots a repeated pattern and decides to build a shared solution.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Internal platforms promise leverage and consistency across teams.
entries.escalates: The platform grows features its users did not request. Adoption is weaker than expected. Teams build workarounds.
entries.ends: The platform absorbs significant engineering time, has thin adoption, and becomes a maintenance liability.

early: Platform language appears before usage evidence.
The team is designing for teams that have not yet asked for help.
The roadmap describes future capabilities, not solved present problems.
mid: Adoption is being pushed rather than pulled.
Users request features the platform cannot support without rework.
The platform team cannot describe which teams depend on them for what.
late: The platform team is large but its impact is unclear.
Consumer teams build around the platform, not on it.
The platform is defended by its creators but not loved by its users.

whyItHappens: Solutionism
Architecture prestige
Weak user feedback loops
Desire to eliminate duplication before proving the pattern
immediateActions: Identify which teams use the platform for what, with evidence
Stop building features with fewer than two confirmed use cases
Define adoption thresholds for continued investment
structuralFixes: Prove repeating need before platforming
Treat internal users with the same rigor as external users
Set explicit criteria for platform graduation from prototype
whatNotToDo: Do not expand platform scope to justify existing investment
Do not treat low adoption as a marketing problem
entries.firstMove: list the concrete product teams currently blocked on the platform, what exactly they need, and whether they have agreed to adopt it on the current timeline.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept downgrading the platform's scope to what two real product teams will use next quarter, or accept that the platform has no customers.
entries.recoveryTrap: rebranding the platform team as an enablement team without changing what they are building.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can accelerate thin-slice prototypes to validate actual demand before full platform investment.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate internal tooling and frameworks too cheaply, encouraging overbuilding before demand is proven.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated platform scaffolding and docs make the platform feel production-ready because the artifacts are polished - hiding that no product team has actually adopted it under load.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Speed of construction is not evidence of need.
entries.commonQuote: Once teams adopt it, they'll see the value.
entries.counterMove: Find three teams who need it today before building for everyone tomorrow.
entries.falsePositive: Some platforms are justified by real repeated need. The failure mode is building before that need is demonstrated.

entries.title: Ownership Drift
entries.summary: Responsibility for systems, services, or decisions becomes unclear over time as teams and structures evolve.
entries.whatItIs: Services, systems, or decisions that once had clear owners lose that clarity through reorgs, growth, or neglect.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when on-call routing or incident response becomes unclear
looksHealthyBecause: everyone contributes across services
team org charts look modern and flexible
incidents get resolved, eventually, by someone
leadership sees no obvious ownership fights

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Hero trap is ownership concentrated on one person. Ownership drift is ownership that isn't on anyone.
Quiet-quitter teams still have clear ownership, just disengaged. Drift loses the ownership itself.
Healthy shared ownership has multiple named owners. Drift has none.

entries.starts: A service or responsibility was clearly owned at creation.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Teams grow, reorg, and focus shifts. Ownership transfer is assumed but rarely formalized.
entries.escalates: Documentation goes stale. On-call rotations become unclear. Incidents bounce between teams.
entries.ends: Something breaks, nobody knows who owns it, and the same people always end up holding the bag.

early: Unclear on-call ownership for some services.
Documentation is stale or absent.
Backlog items for certain systems go untouched.
mid: Incidents require escalation to find the right owner.
Fixes stall because no one feels accountable.
Teams describe services as belonging to a team that no longer exists.
late: Nobody defends quality or roadmap for the service.
The same unowned incidents recur.
The service becomes dangerous to touch and dangerous to ignore.

whyItHappens: Reorgs reassign people but not services
System sprawl outpaces stewardship
Ownership transfer is verbal not formal
Unclear accountability structures
immediateActions: Audit service ownership across all critical systems
Assign a named owner and backup for each service
Identify services with no recent commits, no owner, and active users
structuralFixes: Publish service ownership and keep it current
Make ownership a condition of service operation
Include ownership review in reorg planning
whatNotToDo: Do not accept everyone owns it as an answer
Do not wait for an incident to force the ownership conversation
entries.firstMove: run an audit of the top ten services by blast radius and put a named individual (not a team) next to each one, in writing.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept overloading a few people with formal ownership so that ownership exists at all, then redistribute once it does.
entries.recoveryTrap: publishing an ownership spreadsheet that no one maintains after the initial pass.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help detect orphan services, stale owners, and outdated documentation across large codebases.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can mask drift by auto-answering questions from stale docs, creating an illusion of knowledge where none currently exists.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated service documentation reads as fresh because the prose is fluent, hiding that the docs describe a system state nobody on the current team has actually verified.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Accurate answers from stale sources are still wrong.
entries.commonQuote: I think the platform team owns that, but you might want to check with infrastructure.
entries.counterMove: Every service has a name on it or it does not run.
entries.falsePositive: Collaborative ownership works in small, stable teams. Drift begins when teams change and ownership does not.

entries.title: Synthetic Velocity
entries.summary: Output volume rises sharply while true understanding, maintainability, and durable progress do not.
entries.whatItIs: Teams produce more code, tickets, documents, and visible activity - often assisted by AI - without the expected improvement in outcomes.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when output accelerates before quality controls mature
looksHealthyBecause: PR count, ticket count, and lines of code climb noticeably
engineers report feeling fast and productive
leadership sees visible tool adoption and modern tooling
docs and code comments are more plentiful than before

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Real gains show up in outcomes - fewer incidents, faster learning loops, more durable changes. Synthetic velocity shows up only in output volume.
Ticket theater performs work that was never there. Synthetic velocity produces real artifacts - but of shallower quality than their volume suggests.
Autocomplete architecture is about the shape of generated systems. Synthetic velocity is about the pace and volume of generated work, whatever its shape.

entries.starts: New tooling makes many kinds of work faster to produce.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: The increase in visible activity is real, and some of it is genuinely useful.
entries.escalates: Review becomes shallower than generation. Ownership weakens. Rework and confusion rise slowly rather than dramatically.
entries.ends: The organization confuses artifact volume with actual capability and pays later in defects, drift, and cognitive load.

early: PR volume spikes faster than review capacity.
Generated docs and code proliferate quickly.
People cite speed more often than understanding.
mid: Rework rises quietly.
Code authorship becomes blurry.
The team closes more work while feeling less certain.
late: Incident rate, regression rate, or maintenance burden climbs.
Nobody fully owns some generated areas.
The system looks productive but feels brittle.

whyItHappens: Organizations overvalue visible output
Review and validation do not scale with generation
Productivity claims arrive before operational evidence
AI lowers production cost faster than decision cost
immediateActions: Measure rework, incidents, rollback rate, and review burden
Require clear ownership for generated artifacts
Slow down in high-risk areas
structuralFixes: Treat AI-assisted generation as input, not completion
Upgrade review practices for generated code and docs
Measure durable outcomes instead of raw output
whatNotToDo: Do not ban useful tools reactively
Do not keep celebrating volume while ignoring maintenance cost
entries.firstMove: pick one AI-heavy area and measure a durable outcome (rework rate, incident rate, rollback rate) over the same window as the volume gain.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept that visible output will slow once review, ownership, and testing are held to the same bar as the generation rate.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding more AI tools to review the output of the AI tools.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can still reduce toil and accelerate well-bounded implementation if paired with strong review, ownership, and testing.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: This failure mode is itself AI-amplified: generation becomes cheap, so weak ideas and half-understood changes travel further and faster.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated artifacts look authored - they compile, they read fluently, they pass surface checks - creating the illusion of understood work when no one fully owns or has reasoned through the change.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Cheap output is not cheap system behavior.
entries.commonQuote: We're shipping way faster now.
entries.counterMove: Ask what got easier to produce, and what got easier to trust.
entries.falsePositive: Real productivity gains exist. Synthetic velocity begins when output and understanding separate.

entries.title: Silent Model Drift
entries.summary: Model behavior changes materially in production before the organization notices or responds effectively.
entries.whatItIs: An AI-powered system degrades, shifts, or behaves differently over time due to model, provider, prompt, data, or context changes.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before production, by designing monitoring and evals around expected behavior
looksHealthyBecause: latency and uptime metrics are healthy
error rates look flat
users haven't filed complaints yet
the last release hasn't changed any code

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Benchmark mirage is trusting the wrong measurement. Silent drift is having no measurement at all for the thing that is changing.
Eval Goodhart is optimizing to a fixed target. Drift is the target itself changing without anyone noticing.
Normal variance doesn't require a response. Drift is variance that crossed into a different system behavior.

entries.starts: An AI feature ships and behaves acceptably enough in early use.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Variance is expected, and small quality shifts rarely trigger immediate alarms.
entries.escalates: Prompts change, provider behavior changes, data shifts, or user patterns evolve. Quality declines unevenly.
entries.ends: Trust falls, support load rises, and the organization realizes too late that the system changed before its controls did.

early: Some edge cases feel a bit worse lately.
User complaints are anecdotal and hard to aggregate.
Prompt or context changes are poorly tracked.
mid: Regression patterns appear across similar tasks.
Support gets recurring but weakly classified issues.
Internal confidence drops before formal metrics do.
late: Business errors or user trust issues become visible.
Teams cannot explain when quality started changing.
Rollback options are limited or absent.

whyItHappens: Weak behavioral monitoring
Prompt, retrieval, model, or provider changes lack operational controls
Production evals are immature
Human anecdote arrives before system evidence
immediateActions: Stabilize prompts, versions, and dependencies
Create regression checks around high-risk tasks
Classify support signals into model-behavior patterns
structuralFixes: Run canaries and goldens
Track model, prompt, retrieval, and context versions explicitly
Maintain fallback or degradation paths
whatNotToDo: Do not treat user trust decay as ordinary noise
Do not rely solely on provider assurances
entries.firstMove: pin the prompt, the model version, and every external dependency version - then build a small task-grounded eval you can re-run on a schedule.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept the ongoing operational cost of behavioral evaluation, or accept that you are flying blind on a system whose behavior is non-deterministic.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding more infrastructure dashboards (latency, error rate, token counts) instead of task-quality signals.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help cluster failure cases, generate evaluation sets, and detect subtle answer-pattern changes across production traffic.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: This is an AI-native failure mode: systems whose behavior is partly probabilistic and externally influenced drift silently unless deliberately observed.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI systems can produce fluent, correct-shaped outputs for a long time after underlying behavior has shifted - creating the illusion of stability because the output still looks right even when it isn't.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Behavioral monitoring must reflect task quality, not just uptime or latency.
entries.commonQuote: It's probably just variance.
entries.counterMove: If behavior matters, version it, observe it, and test it like a production dependency.
entries.falsePositive: Some variance is normal. Silent drift is when meaningful change arrives without meaningful control.

entries.title: Autocomplete Architecture
entries.summary: Teams accept AI-suggested structures faster than they understand or own them, embedding design decisions nobody made consciously.
entries.whatItIs: Generated code lands quickly but architectural choices embedded in it are not reviewed, understood, or owned by the team.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before the generated structure becomes load-bearing
looksHealthyBecause: code is generated fast and compiles cleanly
the patterns used look textbook
tests, often also generated, pass
PR throughput is noticeably higher

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Synthetic velocity is about output volume at any scale. Autocomplete architecture is specifically about structural decisions embedded in generated code.
Abstraction addiction is designed on purpose. This is abstraction that nobody actually decided to add.
Legitimate AI-assisted development still runs generated structure through real design judgment. This one skips that step.

entries.starts: A team uses AI to scaffold a feature or service and the output looks reasonable.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: The code works, tests pass, and it saved significant time.
entries.escalates: Patterns from the generated code spread. New code follows the same shape without questioning it. Coupling accumulates.
entries.ends: The architecture has design decisions nobody made, nobody understands fully, and nobody knows how to change safely.

early: Generated code lands without a design discussion.
Architectural patterns spread across the codebase from a generated starting point.
Nobody can explain why the structure is the way it is.
mid: Inconsistent patterns appear because different prompts produced different structures.
Hidden coupling makes changes expensive.
Reviews focus on correctness, not structure.
late: The team avoids changing structural code because nobody is confident.
Incidents have architectural causes nobody anticipated.
New joiners cannot build a mental model of the system.

whyItHappens: Speed of generation outpaces capacity for design review
Weak review practices for generated code
Output looks polished so scrutiny drops
Architecture decisions are embedded implicitly in generated code
immediateActions: Require explanation of any generated structural decision before it lands
Review generated code for structure, not just correctness
Trace the origin of any repeated structural pattern
structuralFixes: Make architectural review a condition of generated code merging
Maintain a lightweight architecture decision record
Distinguish implementation generation from design generation
whatNotToDo: Do not ban AI code generation
Do not let correctness substitute for comprehension
entries.firstMove: pick one recent generated PR that introduced a new abstraction, interface, or pattern - and require the author to explain why that structure exists before it merges.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept slower generated-code throughput so each structural choice passes through real review.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding lint rules or style guides, which catch surface shape but not structural decisions.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can still accelerate implementation significantly once design choices are made explicitly and consciously.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: Native mode: AI scales questionable design instantly across a codebase before anyone has reviewed the implicit decisions embedded in the output.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Generated code compiles, follows named patterns, and passes its also-generated tests - which makes the resulting architecture look like a deliberate design when no one actually designed it.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Passing tests do not validate architectural choices.
entries.commonQuote: The AI just scaffolded it, it's fine.
entries.counterMove: Understand the structure before you inherit it.
entries.falsePositive: Generated code can be excellent. The failure mode is adopting embedded design decisions without examining them.

entries.title: The Benchmark Mirage
entries.summary: Model selection or evaluation is guided by benchmark performance that does not reflect real production behavior.
entries.whatItIs: A team selects, tunes, or presents a model based on benchmark scores that do not map to the tasks, formats, or edge cases that matter in production.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before a model is selected based primarily on published benchmarks
looksHealthyBecause: benchmarks are published by credible organizations
scores improve steadily over model versions
marketing materials cite consistent leaderboards
leadership hears "this is the best model in our category"

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Eval Goodhart is your own eval becoming the target. Benchmark mirage is trusting someone else's eval as the target.
Drift is real change you can't see. Mirage is measurement that never matched your reality in the first place.
Rigorous selection starts from the task. Mirage starts from the leaderboard.

entries.starts: A team needs to choose or evaluate a model and reaches for published benchmark scores.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Benchmarks are produced by credible sources and offer a consistent comparison point.
entries.escalates: The chosen model performs well in demos but struggles with real user tasks, edge cases, or production formats.
entries.ends: Trust erodes, performance is inconsistent, and the team realizes the eval never matched the use case.

early: Model selection discussions center on benchmark rankings.
No task-specific evaluation exists.
Demo inputs are carefully selected and not representative.
mid: Good benchmark model underperforms on real tasks.
Users encounter failure modes that were not tested.
The team cannot explain the gap between benchmark and production.
late: Trust in the AI feature declines despite strong benchmark numbers.
Significant effort goes into prompting around model weaknesses.
The team re-evaluates the model selection with evidence that should have existed before launch.

whyItHappens: Benchmarks are accessible and published
Task-level evaluation is expensive to build
Stakeholder pressure favors legible comparisons
Demo conditions do not reproduce production conditions
immediateActions: Build a small evaluation set from real or representative production inputs
Run shadow testing with real user tasks before committing
Include human review of representative outputs in evaluation
structuralFixes: Maintain a domain-specific evaluation harness
Require task-grounded evidence for model selection decisions
Separate benchmark review from production readiness review
whatNotToDo: Do not dismiss benchmarks entirely
Do not treat provider evaluation as neutral
entries.firstMove: build a small evaluation set from your actual production inputs before you run a single external benchmark.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept the upfront cost of authoring a task-grounded eval, or accept that your model choice is being driven by other people's problems.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding more external benchmarks to triangulate, which deepens the illusion of rigor without touching the task gap.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help generate scenario-based eval sets if the scenarios are carefully curated from real production patterns.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI-native by definition: the failure mode is built into how models are marketed and selected.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Benchmark scores produce clean numeric comparisons that feel like rigorous evidence - hiding that the benchmark may not correlate with your task at all.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A model that wins benchmarks you did not set may be optimized for problems you do not have.
entries.commonQuote: It scored highest on MMLU, so it should be the best choice.
entries.whoSaysThis: product lead or ai engineer in a model selection discussion
entries.counterMove: Build the eval before you pick the model.
entries.falsePositive: Benchmarks provide useful signal. The mirage begins when they substitute for task-grounded evidence.

entries.title: RAG Without Ground Truth
entries.summary: A retrieval-augmented system is built and deployed before source quality, citation reliability, and answer validation are established.
entries.whatItIs: A RAG pipeline retrieves from untrusted or unvalidated sources and presents confident answers without ground-truth verification.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before the system is presented to users as authoritative
looksHealthyBecause: answers include citations and sound authoritative
demos impress stakeholders and pass without pushback
retrieval returns plausible-looking results every time
the system never says "I don't know"

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Drift is change over time. This one is launched broken.
Context hoarding adds content hoping quality improves. RAG without ground truth is the same impulse at retrieval time.
Real RAG has validated sources, adversarial evals, and explicit don't-know paths. Everything else is a demo in production.

entries.starts: A team builds a retrieval system over a corpus of documents and it produces fluent, plausible answers.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Answers look good and demos are impressive.
entries.escalates: Answers are confidently wrong. Sources are stale, out of scope, or misinterpreted. Users trust the system more than they should.
entries.ends: A significant error surfaces publicly or operationally. The system loses credibility that is hard to recover.

early: Source trust and freshness are vague.
Evaluation is based on demos rather than systematic testing.
No human expert has reviewed representative outputs.
mid: Users encounter confident wrong answers.
Citations point to sources that do not support the claim.
Edge cases and out-of-scope questions are handled poorly.
late: User distrust becomes explicit.
Support receives escalations about false information.
The team cannot explain which sources are authoritative.

whyItHappens: Source quality is ignored in favor of fluency
Evaluation is weak or demo-based
No ground-truth corpus was built before deployment
Hallucination risk is underestimated
immediateActions: Define and publish the trusted source list
Add citation requirements and source verification
Build a hallucination test set from known facts in the domain
structuralFixes: Maintain a curated, versioned ground-truth corpus
Run regular answer quality audits with domain experts
Build fallback responses for out-of-scope queries
whatNotToDo: Do not treat fluency as accuracy
Do not deploy without domain expert sign-off on representative outputs
entries.firstMove: define what the system should refuse to answer, and test that behavior before you test what it can answer.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept narrower scope - fewer answers, more refusals - so the answers given are defensible.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding more sources to the index, which increases surface area for plausible-sounding but ungrounded output.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can improve discoverability and summarization if retrieval and validation are disciplined.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI makes plausible-sounding nonsense feel authoritative and confident, which is the core risk of this failure mode.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Fluent, citation-backed answers read as authoritative regardless of whether retrieval surfaced the right documents - creating confidence indistinguishable from correctness without an eval the system doesn't have.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: A fluent answer is not a correct answer.
entries.commonQuote: The demo looks great. It's pulling from our docs.
entries.whoSaysThis: product manager or ai engineer after a demo
entries.counterMove: Ask what happens when it does not know the answer before asking what happens when it does.
entries.falsePositive: RAG is a powerful pattern. The failure mode is deploying it without ground truth, not using it.

entries.title: Prompt Ops Chaos
entries.summary: Prompts, model settings, and hidden instructions change without version control, making system behavior unpredictable and undebuggable.
entries.whatItIs: The instruction layer of an AI system - prompts, system messages, retrieval configuration, tool definitions - changes without the controls applied to other production code.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: before the first production prompt is written without version control
looksHealthyBecause: prompts iterate quickly based on user feedback
the team speaks fluent "prompt engineering"
fixes ship same-day in response to user complaints
behavior improvements are demoed frequently

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: That is about accepted structures in code. This is about accepted instructions in prompts.
Drift is behavior changing because the model or world changed. Prompt chaos is behavior changing because the team changed the prompt without saying.
Healthy iteration is versioned, tested, and reversible. Chaos is none of those.

entries.starts: Prompts live in a shared doc, a config file, or someone's head.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Prompts feel more like text than code and iteration is fast.
entries.escalates: Behavior changes unexpectedly. Nobody can reproduce the good version. Debugging requires archaeology.
entries.ends: A behavior change causes a production incident and the team cannot explain when or why the prompt changed.

early: Prompts live in chat threads, docs, or config files with no history.
Changes to prompts are not tracked alongside code changes.
Different environments use different prompts without documentation.
mid: Behavior changes cannot be traced to a specific change.
Testing a prompt change requires manual comparison.
The team argues about what the current prompt says.
late: A production issue is traced to an undocumented prompt change.
Rollback is not possible because no previous version was saved.
Debugging requires reconstructing history from conversations.

whyItHappens: Poor versioning discipline
Prompts are treated as text, not configuration
Low observability of AI system internals
Fast iteration norms override operational controls
immediateActions: Move all prompts into version-controlled files immediately
Log the active prompt version alongside every production request
Create a change log for any prompt modification
structuralFixes: Version prompts, models, tools, and context windows together
Build eval runs that run automatically on prompt changes
Treat prompt changes as production deployments
whatNotToDo: Do not allow prompts to live outside version control
Do not iterate on production prompts without staging validation
entries.firstMove: move every production prompt into a version-controlled file today, even if the prompt stays identical - baseline first, iterate second.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept slower prompt iteration in exchange for prompt changes that are traceable, reviewable, and reversible.
entries.recoveryTrap: adding an eval harness before the prompt is under version control, which reports on a moving target.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help diff prompt variants and summarize behavioral differences if prompts are tracked.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI systems are uniquely vulnerable to invisible behavioral changes because so much behavior is encoded in the instruction layer rather than in deterministic code.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: Prompt changes feel like minor wording tweaks, not code changes - creating the illusion that they are low-risk even though they can silently shift system behavior.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Configuration drift is still drift even when it lives in a prompt.
entries.commonQuote: I just tweaked the prompt a bit, it should be fine.
entries.counterMove: If you changed the prompt, you changed the system.
entries.falsePositive: Prompt iteration is healthy. The failure mode is iteration without control.

entries.title: Premature Scaling
entries.summary: Teams design and build for scale that has not arrived, creating unnecessary complexity before product-market fit is established.
entries.whatItIs: Significant infrastructure, architectural, or operational complexity is introduced to handle load, volume, or scale that does not yet exist and may never exist in that form.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when scaling is discussed before current load is actually constraining delivery
looksHealthyBecause: the architecture looks modern and distributed
the team cites documented best practices
leadership hears "built for scale"
early performance benchmarks look strong

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Platform-before-product builds an internal product for nobody. Premature scaling builds infrastructure for a load nobody is putting on it.
Abstraction addiction is future-proofing the code. Premature scaling is future-proofing the runtime.
Responsible planning designs for the next order of magnitude. Premature scaling designs for the final one.

entries.starts: A team anticipates growth and designs the system to handle it.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Retrofitting scale later is genuinely expensive, and the team has seen or heard about systems that did not survive success.
entries.escalates: Operational complexity multiplies. A small team maintains distributed infrastructure designed for a system ten times larger.
entries.ends: Velocity slows, the system is expensive to run, and the scale never arrived or arrived in a form the infrastructure did not anticipate.

early: Scale conversations happen before product validation.
Architecture discussions reference companies at orders-of-magnitude larger scale.
Complexity is introduced to handle theoretical load.
mid: The system is significantly harder to run than the business problem demands.
Engineers spend more time on infrastructure than product.
Onboarding to the system takes far longer than contributing to it.
late: The scale never came, or came in a shape that required a redesign anyway.
Infrastructure cost exceeds business value at current scale.
The team cannot change product direction quickly because the architecture assumes a specific load model.

whyItHappens: Survivorship bias from high-scale case studies
Architecture prestige
Genuine but premature extrapolation of growth
Fear of being the engineer who did not plan for scale
immediateActions: Measure current load and define what would actually require architectural change
Identify complexity that serves anticipated rather than real load
Simplify the system to match current operating reality
structuralFixes: Set explicit scale thresholds before scaling architecture
Make the simple path the default until evidence demands otherwise
Treat complexity as a cost, not a quality signal
whatNotToDo: Do not optimize for load that has not been observed
Do not conflate architectural sophistication with engineering quality
entries.firstMove: measure current real load and write down the load at which the current simple architecture would actually break - most systems are nowhere near it.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept a system that will need meaningful rework if it wins the market, so the team stays alive long enough to reach that problem.
entries.recoveryTrap: simplifying one layer of the scale architecture while keeping the rest, which preserves the operational burden.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can model current load, project realistic growth curves, and help identify which components genuinely need scaling investment.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate sophisticated distributed architecture quickly, making premature scaling feel cheap and easy to justify.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI can scaffold a sophisticated distributed architecture in hours, making premature scaling feel low-cost to start - the real cost lives in operating it for years, which is invisible at generation time.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: The cost of premature scaling is not in writing the code but in operating it.
entries.commonQuote: We need to design this to handle a million users from day one.
entries.whoSaysThis: architect or senior engineer in a design review
entries.counterMove: Design for the next order of magnitude, not the final one.
entries.falsePositive: Some systems genuinely require scale from the start. The failure mode is building for scale before the product exists.

entries.title: Interface Contract Neglect
entries.summary: APIs, events, and data contracts between teams degrade silently as systems evolve without formal contract management.
entries.whatItIs: The interfaces between systems or teams are assumed rather than specified, versioned, or tested, and they degrade over time without anyone noticing until something breaks.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when a second team begins consuming an interface for the first time
entries.oftenMistakenAs: an isolated bug or integration issue
looksHealthyBecause: both teams can describe the integration informally
the integration worked the last time it was tested
no one has filed a contract-related incident yet
the interface looks simple from either end

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Dependency fog is unclear what you depend on. Contract neglect is clear dependency with unclear contract shape.
Ownership drift is about the service owner. Contract neglect is about the contract owner - often different or missing.
Normal coordination names a contract. Neglect lets two teams hold different versions of it in their heads.

entries.starts: Two teams share an interface that was initially defined informally.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: The contract is known to both teams, and changing it requires coordination.
entries.escalates: One team changes internal behavior. The other team's assumptions break silently or loudly. Nobody owns the contract.
entries.ends: A production incident reveals that the interface had changed, been misunderstood, or accumulated incompatible assumptions on both sides.

early: APIs are documented in a README that is not kept current.
Interface changes are communicated verbally.
No consumer tests exist for the contract.
mid: Integration issues appear repeatedly at release time.
One team is surprised by a change the other team considers minor.
Different teams have different understandings of what the interface guarantees.
late: A production incident is caused by a contract mismatch.
Debugging requires archaeology across both teams' code.
Fear of changing interfaces causes both teams to accumulate workarounds.

whyItHappens: Interfaces are treated as internal rather than owned
Contract testing is seen as overhead
Teams prioritize their own velocity over integration stability
No single owner exists for the interface
immediateActions: Document and publish the current interface contract
Add consumer-driven contract tests
Establish a change notification process for interface modifications
structuralFixes: Version interfaces explicitly
Assign interface ownership to a named person or team
Treat breaking changes as deployments with impact assessments
whatNotToDo: Do not treat absence of complaints as evidence the contract is sound
Do not allow interface changes without downstream notification
entries.firstMove: write down the current contract - exact fields, types, semantics, error shapes - and have both sides sign off before the next change.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept slower interface changes in exchange for changes that both sides stay compatible with.
entries.recoveryTrap: introducing a schema registry with no enforcement, which documents drift instead of preventing it.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can help document existing contracts, generate consumer tests, and diff interface versions to surface incompatibilities.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI can generate new interface implementations quickly without checking compatibility with existing consumers, accelerating contract drift.
entries.aiFalseConfidence: AI-generated interface implementations compile cleanly on each side, creating the illusion of compatibility when the two sides may actually be generating against different assumed contracts.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated implementations may be internally consistent but externally incompatible.
entries.commonQuote: We didn't think that change would affect anyone else.
entries.counterMove: If two systems share a boundary, someone owns the contract.
entries.falsePositive: Some interface changes are minor and well-communicated. The failure mode is treating all interfaces as internal details.

entries.title: The Quiet Quitter Team
entries.summary: A team stops raising risks, pushing back on decisions, or flagging problems - appearing harmonious while actually disengaged.
entries.whatItIs: A team has learned that raising concerns, dissenting, or flagging problems does not change outcomes, and has stopped doing so.
entries.bestMomentToIntervene: when a team stops generating disagreement or surface-level conflict entirely
looksHealthyBecause: standups are short and calm
there are no escalations or visible conflict
delivery still looks steady
survey scores are polite

easilyConfusedWith.distinction: Drift is unclear ownership. Quiet-quitter teams often have clear ownership and simply nobody pushing on it.
In a hero trap, one person is over-engaged. In a quiet-quitter team, nobody is.
A high-functioning team disagrees and resolves. A quiet-quitter team does not disagree.

entries.starts: A team raises concerns that are dismissed, overridden, or penalized. This happens more than once.
entries.feelsReasonableBecause: Absence of conflict looks like trust and alignment.
entries.escalates: The team executes orders without input, stops flagging risks, and withdraws from problem-solving.
entries.ends: Preventable failures occur. Key people leave. The manager discovers the team had known about problems for months.

early: Retrospectives produce safe feedback only.
No one pushes back in planning.
Questions from leadership are answered with agreement.
mid: Known risks are not escalated.
Problems are fixed silently without being surfaced.
Team engagement in decision-making drops quietly.
late: Attrition begins among the most capable.
A significant failure occurs that several people knew was coming.
Post-mortems reveal that concerns existed but were not raised.

whyItHappens: Previous feedback was penalized or ignored
Psychological safety is low
Management rewards compliance over candor
Team has learned that input does not change outcomes
immediateActions: Run a skip-level or anonymous survey to surface real sentiment
Demonstrate that feedback changes a decision publicly and quickly
Create a low-stakes channel for raising concerns
structuralFixes: Reward candor explicitly and visibly
Close the loop on feedback in writing
Train managers to detect and respond to disengagement signals
whatNotToDo: Do not run engagement surveys without acting on results
Do not interpret silence as satisfaction
entries.firstMove: have a skip-level or anonymous conversation with the team specifically about what they have stopped bringing up.
entries.hardTradeoff: accept surfacing conflict and discomfort now, or accept that the next surprise will be a larger one.
entries.recoveryTrap: running another engagement survey, which the team will answer the same polite way as the last one.
aiCanHelpWith: AI can analyze patterns in communication, code review comments, and written feedback to surface declining engagement signals.
aiCanMakeWorseBy: AI-generated summaries of team sentiment can produce falsely positive pictures if the underlying signal is passive compliance rather than genuine engagement.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Polite language in surveys and stand-ups does not mean the team is engaged.
entries.commonQuote: The team seems really aligned lately.
entries.counterMove: If no one is pushing back, find out why before assuming it is confidence.
